sentences.text: Belgium was invaded May the 15th.
The 17th, it was all occupied.
The king surrendered [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] May 28, 1940.
Did things begin to change immediately?
Yes.
Because the Germans kept on saying whatever we're changing is only-- we're-- don't worry.
We're just occupying to go past through Belgium.
We're not going to stay in Belgium.
And in the beginning, I think a lot of people believed them.
And so did a lot of Jews because they went on with their business and they-- with their life.
But it didn't take too long, and they started doing what they did in Germany.
They started systematically taking away everything from the Jews.
For example, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said that the Jewish stores can't be open a certain time.
And to make sure that they know that it's a Jewish store, doors at the store said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jews, Jude.

And the other stores, some of the, the Belgium people-- and a lot of them collaborated with them-- wrote down, 'Dogs and Jews not allowed.' So in a store where I used to go and buy candy or bread, all at once there was a sign.
I can't go in there anymore.
And then they wanted to catch Jews and get money out of them.
I remember my daddy getting a, a ticket.
And the ticket said, you played cards in middle of the Keyserlei.
And I remember one of the men who got a notice like that went to the Stadt House where-- the Germans were there.
And he was beaten up and taken money.
And so my mother took the  note.
And I can see her doing it right now.
And she tore it in pieces.
And she said, you're not going.
And he didn't go.
Yes, I was.
In the public school, the children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we, we weren't anymore friends.
We were like poison.
It wasn't-- they didn't come near us.
They didn't speak to us.
But when I was given notice by the teacher that we-- the Jews can't come to school anymore, I still felt that I had a friend, a non-Jewish friend that I used to go to school with them.
And we used to buy apples together, and everything.
And when-- after I was at home maybe a week, I decided to go to her home.
And she lived in a beautiful place not too far from me.
And I went there.
And she was in her yard.
And it was pretty-- she saw me coming.
And she shouted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very, very loud that the whole world should hear her.
Clara, don't come near me!
I can't play with you anymore!
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went home.
I cried bitterly.
And I-- and my mother said, don't worry.
She'll play with you again.
I have to admit that I have never gone near her, even after the war when I went back to Belgium.
And I have never seen her.
But I know where she lives.
I knew-- and I know she's in Antwerp.
But that was it.
My best non-Jewish friend became against me.
I became poison.
Now who told her that?
And I, many times now think about it.
And I think that my answer is pretty clear-- her parents.
Because she was too young to have listened to anybody else.
But  her parents say, you can't play anymore with Jewish children.
And she couldn't play anymore with me.
I was not anymore a friend.
It was all on the radio.
You heard on the radio.
You heard-- pamphlets, whatever you-- the papers, it was all full of what Germany is and what the Jews are.
And the Jews are no good.
They are like the museum said, mice.
And they are-- they're not human beings.
I remember very well-- I couldn't go to public school anymore, but I was still in Hebrew school.
And I had to go to Hebrew school.
But it was a birthday party of one of my friends.
And my mother said, you'll go to the party after Hebrew school.
I played hooky.
I decided no, I'm going-- instead of to Hebrew school, I'm going to go to my friend.
When I came home, I found the living room-- and I still see it-- with some of my friends' parents crying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as soon as I open the door, my mother came over and hugged me and kissed me.
And it was very, very emotional, dramatic.
And I, I didn't know what it was.
And then my other friend's parents told me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] that the Germans had taken a truck and went to the Hebrew school and filled the truck with all the children who were there.
And they were taken away.
And I never saw my other Hebrew-school friends, my other Jewish friends again.
They was taken away to Auschwitz.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother always used to say, my [NON-ENGLISH], she knew when not to go to school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe I did.
And who knows why?
When did you first find out that there were people that were not supporting the Nazis, that were helping to work against them?
We didn't find that out until we were in hiding.
I think she was.
But I never, during hiding, had anything to do with her.
But I think, by this leaflet that she was giving us, that she was not one-- or a good friend of the Germans.
I think she was with the Underground.
There weren't too many.
No, I don't.
I know I, I should remember it.
We'll look it up.
[LAUGHS]

As I was saying before, first, the Jewish star; no outside for Jews after a certain hour; no radios; no doctors could treat non-Jews; bankers-- it was systematically taking away every right of the Jewish people who were living in Belgium.
And then, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one-- the Germans always started on Friday nights.
Whatever they wanted to do with the Jewish people was Friday night.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One Friday night, we got a, a notice that if the boys-- my brother wasn't bar mitzvah yet, he was 12.
So--
My older brother.
If he would go to, to work for the Germans, then we would stay.
They wouldn't touch us.
And it took a week.
They all were discussing it, he with his friends and the parents.
And my sister said, don't go.
I said, you-- maybe you should go.
Maybe you shouldn't go.
We all kept on talking and talking.
And the Germans in the note said, if your son goes-- the oldest son in the house goes, then he will come back every week.
He's just going to help us work for the German machinery.
We need hands.
And my brother finally decided to go--
--with his friends.
Because his friends were going.
And he kept on saying, I'm saving my family.
And nothing is going to be wrong with me.
So I'll work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, my brother left our home with [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a piece of luggage.
My mother packed it real beautiful-- clothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My daddy gave him a diamond ring to put on his finger, just in case he'll need something.
He said, sell the ring and get whatever you need.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he opened the door.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really don't remember seeing him walk out of the door.
I don't know if I wanted to look or-- maybe I saw it, but I want to forget it.
And he never came back again.
Later on, we received a postcard from him that he had thrown out from the cattle cars, saying, they didn't need us for work.
We are going to concentration camps.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I never saw my brother again, neither did any of us.
It was in 1941, before they started taking all of us, before they came to take all the Jews.
It was a few months before.
They took us in '42.
I mean, not me.
I went in hiding in '42.
But those that they were able to catch and take, they went in '42.
As a matter of fact, I have-- I was in Auschwitz in 1982, was sent by the-- from Washington.
And I lectured there.
And there's a room there that the Germans kept systematic the numbers of everybody they killed, the date when they brought in the person, and the date when they killed the person.
My students, who were international students, after we saw Auschwitz-- the ovens and all of the other things, the barracks-- went in there.
And they saw me staying outside.
And they said, don't you want to come in, Mrs.
Isaacman?
And I said, no.
They meant well.
When they went in there, they looked at the register.
And there are thousands and thousands of names there.
And they found Heshie Heller.
And they brought me out a piece of paper with the number that they had tattooed my brother, and with the date when he came into Auschwitz, and with the date when they put him in the oven.
The next day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we went in Auschwitz and in Birkenau.
And we went into the rooms where the ovens were.
And I really thought that after speaking so much about the Holocaust and seeing movies and films and everything else, nothing will touch me anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I put my hand on the oven, and I really felt my brother's hand pulling me.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then realized that nobody, nobody can think that they really know or they know everything about what the Holocaust is, even those like myself, who went through it as a hidden child.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think that I'm telling this story right now-- very seldom that I'm telling this-- is because of my brother.
There's nobody else to, to speak for him.
So I'm here to speak for him.
By the way, I like you to know that the first 20 years after the Holocaust, nobody knew that I was a survivor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I came to America in 1946.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My husband was 19.
I was a little younger.
I wanted to go to have an education.
I went to temple.
And it bothered me that wherever I went, wherever school I went, I never heard anybody mentioning the Holocaust.
And nobody-- and when I asked my Danny, my husband, are they speaking at university about it or so?
He used to say, it's taboo.
Nobody speaks about it.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think after all of this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still didn't want to talk about it.
There was something there that just didn't let me do it.
I was encouraged by Elie Wiesel to speak and to write.
And I-- if it's OK, I'd like to tell a very short story how it happened.
My husband was president of Gratz College.
And he used to share platforms with Elie Wiesel.
And we were at an international conference of Catholics.
And there were only three Jews-- Elie, Danny, my husband, and myself, who went there just for fun.
And we were having dinner.
The rest were-- and my husband said to Elie, tell her to write.
And he said, what should she write?
And my husband said, my wife is a survivor.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he said to me, write.
I said, you have written for all of us.
What can I say that you haven't said yet?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had a long conversation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, what you can write, I can't.
And what I can write, you can't.
So go home and write.
And he gave me instructions what to do.
And I came home, didn't want to do it.
But I had a very wonderful man who said, you're not going to bed before you do it.
And I started writing.
And that's how I started telling my story and the story of my family and about the Holocaust itself.
Our family, I'm sorry.
Oh, I thought you had-- my family's way of life was affected that we started feeling, all of us, even those of us who didn't say it.
And my mother and father used to speak that we shouldn't hear.
I guess they didn't want to scare us too much.
It was hush, hush when we heard, I mean, when we were listening.

But they realized, and we realized-- I, right away, with my education that I had on the Zionist movement-- realized that what they taught me, they didn't want to tell me straight what it means to be a Jew.
But they told me, they taught me what it means to be a Jew.
And my Hebrew school taught me.
And the Bible taught me.
But then I realized what it meant to be a Jew.
And I knew that I am different.
I can't do what everybody else can do.
And I kept on asking, why?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I kept on saying, am I not the same?
Don't I have the same-- like a little child, like a kindergarten kid.
I have two eyes.
I have a heart.
I have a mind.
I have ears.
What's different from me to them?
And what did I do wrong to those people who hate me, who don't even know me?
And I started feeling hatred.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I walked in the streets with my star, I felt bigotry.
I felt everything that is wrong in the world.
And I started feeling very sad that because I was born into the Jewish people, into my Jewish religion, I became so different to the world.
And they don't even know me.
And then we all started feeling it very strongly.
Sadly enough, I still feel it.
I couldn't do anything anymore.
I mean, everything I did was thinking, hey, are they going to see it?
Are they going to know it?
Am I allowed to do this?
Even if I had rules, I still-- I still felt-- I made myself more rules and more feeling.
And it wasn't-- the world wasn't mine anymore.
It was like I was living from day to day.

But looking back, I still lived, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] quote, unquote, 'a normal life.' I still had my own room, my own house, my parents around me, not my brother, but my sister and my younger brother.
And I still lived.
My father had his business, but it was only between the Jews.
As a matter of fact, they had once-- my father went to the bourse, to the diamond bourse.
And he came home.
And he said, I'm lucky I'm here.
The Germans walked into the diamond bourse.
And they emptied every pocket from all the Jews in there.
You could find, they threw-- because the Jews saw who were coming in, they threw the diamonds through the windows.
You could find diamonds on the streets of Antwerp.
They threw diamonds in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a plant here, in the plant things.
And my dad told us that everybody emptied their pockets.
Everybody threw everything away that they shouldn't find anything on them.
Yes, but he closed it after a while.
He had nothing after a while.
Well, he had enough money still to go in someplace and buy.
And then he had-- a few here and there, a friend, a non-Jew, who, for a little money, would go and buy and bring-- so you paid them.
But they at least got it.
He, he had put away, I guess, enough.
Because my daddy-- just before they round us up, my daddy decided to put away packages of diamonds.
It was one or two, I don't know.
And he called us all together one day.
And he went to the door.
And he took the wood off from the door, one piece of wood.
And he made a hole.
And he put in diamonds there, package of diamonds.
And he said, children, I want you to know that if you ever need something, it's here-- if we get out of the house.
I guess by then, he felt that they're going to round us up.
They're going to take us.
But he didn't want to say it.
And he put that wood back.
And we knew where we had diamonds.
And, by the way, he gave us all a ring, a diamond ring to carry, to have with us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That package of diamonds has caused quite a story after we were in hiding.
When I'll get to the hiding, I will tell the story about the package of diamonds.
In 1942-- I think it was July.
I don't remember exactly.
And they-- and again, it was a Friday night.
And that Friday night-- everything will stay with me.
But that Friday night will never, never [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And after so many years, I still have nightmares.
Once or twice a year, I dream.
It was pleasant when my husband was around, and I used to get up and scream.
So we used to sing.
He had a way of, how many languages we can sing songs in bed in middle of the night?
But now it's not so pleasant.
Because I get up, and I am alone in bed.
And, and, and I-- the only thing is, I jump out of bed.
I change my security.
And I start walking as big as the house is.
It was a Friday night.
And it was blackout.
I think that we should all know about it.
And I'm going to try and describe it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The war was on, of course.
My daddy had just come back from synagogue.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were all, the rest of us, home.
In the dining room, there was one empty chair, my brother's.
Nobody ever sat in that chair.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The table was set beautiful.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all at once, we heard trucks.
Now, I was used to hear those trucks.
But this time, the trucks stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And we were wondering, why did they stop?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they stopped.
Now, we would have loved to look out.
But my daddy didn't let us get up from the table.
We're going to stop here and come back to this.
